topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and experimentally demonstrate a real-time, closed-loop quantum error correction protocol on a superconducting distance-3 surface code that integrates recursive Bayesian online noise parameter estimation with a low-latency, machine-learning-based adaptive decoder operating within qubit coherence times.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) decoders typically operate on static noise models and fail to adapt to time-dependent, correlated, and non-Markovian noise prevalent in NISQ devices, limiting logical qubit fidelity. While machine-learning (ML) decoders and noise estimation techniques have been studied separately or offline, no existing work demonstrates their tight real-time integration with continuous noise tracking and feedback at latencies compatible with superconducting qubit coherence windows.

APPROACH:  
We implement a small-distance surface code on a superconducting qubit platform with high-fidelity ancilla-based syndrome extraction and real-time syndrome streaming. A recursive Bayesian inference module continuously updates a noise model each QEC cycle using measured physical qubit parameters (T1, T2, crosstalk) and syndrome data. This updated noise model feeds a lightweight ML decoder—such as a shallow recurrent neural network with embedded Bayesian parameter updates—that predicts error configurations and prescribes corrections within microsecond-scale latencies. Both modules run on classical co-processors tightly integrated with the quantum hardware to maintain total feedback latency below qubit coherence times.

EXPERIMENT:  
We benchmark logical error rates under native and artificially modulated noise conditions by comparing the adaptive ML decoder’s performance against a baseline minimum-weight perfect matching decoder operating on a fixed noise model. Logical qubit fidelity is quantified through repeated projective measurements after multiple QEC cycles, while injection of controlled noise fluctuations tests the noise tracking and adaptive decoding efficacy.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines real-time Bayesian noise tracking with an ultra-low-latency, ML-based decoder in a closed-loop architecture demonstrated on physical hardware, overcoming the engineering and algorithmic challenges of integrating adaptive QEC within coherence time constraints—an advance not yet realized in prior experimental or simulation studies.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Implement and experimentally validate a closed-loop quantum error correction protocol on a distance-3 surface code in NISQ devices that integrates classical error correction on syndrome measurement outcomes with an adaptive decoder periodically updated using interleaved noise spectroscopy data to mitigate imperfect syndrome measurements and correlated noise.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) demonstrations often assume ideal or static noise models and perfect syndrome measurements, limiting their effectiveness on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware where measurement errors and correlated, time-varying noise prevail. Developing hardware-aware, adaptive QEC protocols that incorporate realistic syndrome measurement imperfections and non-Markovian noise remains an open challenge critical to improving logical qubit fidelity on near-term devices.

APPROACH:  
We propose to apply classical error-correcting codes (e.g., repetition or Hamming codes) directly to syndrome measurement outcomes to reduce readout errors, combined with an adaptive decoding strategy that updates its noise model parameters periodically based on noise spectroscopy data gathered from interleaved calibration sequences during the experiment. This closed-loop approach enables the decoder to account for temporal noise correlations and measurement imperfections, producing more accurate error correction tailored to current device conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
On a 9–25 qubit superconducting or trapped-ion platform, repeatedly implement distance-3 surface code cycles with calibrated multi-qubit parity measurements; interleave noise spectroscopy sequences (Ramsey/echo) for temporal noise characterization; post-process syndrome bits with classical error correction; and decode adaptively using updated noise models. Benchmark logical error rates per QEC cycle against baseline decoding assuming perfect syndrome data and static noise to assess fidelity improvements.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely demonstrates the integration of classical error correction on syndrome bits with a hardware-aware, adaptive decoder updated via live noise spectroscopy in a closed feedback loop during QEC operation—surpassing prior approaches that treat syndrome errors and noise models as static or idealized and establishing a practical pathway toward robust QEC tailored to real-time NISQ device noise characteristics.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and experimentally demonstrate a closed-loop, noise-adaptive syndrome extraction protocol combined with machine learning decoders trained on device-specific, correlated non-Markovian noise profiles to achieve >10% logical error rate reduction on small surface code patches in current NISQ hardware.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction schemes on NISQ devices largely rely on static syndrome extraction circuits and decoders assuming uncorrelated, Markovian noise, which fail to capture realistic error correlations such as crosstalk and leakage. These simplifications limit logical qubit performance and obscure the potential of adaptive control to mitigate correlated errors in near-term hardware with limited connectivity and gate fidelity.

APPROACH:  
We propose to first characterize detailed temporal and spatial noise correlations, including crosstalk and leakage, via dynamical decoupling and multi-qubit benchmarking on superconducting or trapped-ion platforms. Using this data, we iteratively optimize syndrome extraction circuits at the pulse-shaping and gate scheduling level to minimize error propagation, implementing a closed-loop feedback protocol that refines these parameters based on live syndrome measurement outcomes. Concurrently, we develop ML decoders—e.g., graph neural networks—trained on experimentally inferred correlated noise models to improve decoding accuracy beyond traditional independent-error assumptions.

EXPERIMENT:  
The method will be validated by implementing both standard and noise-adaptive syndrome extraction circuits on 5–17 qubit surface code patches, collecting syndrome data and logical error rates under matched calibration conditions. Logical qubit lifetimes and decoder performance metrics will be compared statistically to confirm a target >10% reduction in logical errors from the integrated adaptive approach.

NOVELTY:  
This is the first experimentally grounded framework that integrates detailed correlated, non-Markovian noise spectroscopy with real-time, closed-loop adaptive syndrome extraction circuit optimization at the hardware control level, combined with ML decoders trained on realistic noise models, providing a concrete, scalable path to improved quantum error correction on near-term devices.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
We propose and experimentally demonstrate a closed-loop, real-time adaptive syndrome extraction protocol for small-distance surface codes on NISQ devices that dynamically optimizes syndrome measurement frequency using continuous diagnostics of qubit coherence, syndrome fidelity, and correlated noise, explicitly accounting for measurement backaction and classical feedback latency.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) protocols on NISQ hardware rely on fixed-frequency syndrome extraction schedules and often assume Markovian noise with negligible feedback latency, which overlooks measurement-induced errors, crosstalk, and realistic classical processing delays that degrade logical fidelity. There is a critical need to optimize syndrome extraction strategies under realistic, temporally and spatially correlated noise to improve logical error suppression on near-term quantum devices.

APPROACH:  
We implement an adaptive control algorithm—such as a lightweight model-predictive controller or Bayesian optimizer—that continuously updates syndrome extraction intervals from streaming measurements of qubit \(T_1\), \(T_2\), syndrome measurement fidelities, and multi-qubit error correlations. The controller switches among a discrete set of precompiled syndrome extraction frequencies during distance-3 surface code runs to balance error accumulation against measurement-induced decoherence while respecting classical feedback latency constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
We perform calibration to characterize coherence times, syndrome fidelities, correlated error rates, and feedback latencies on a superconducting or trapped-ion platform, then compare logical error rates obtained from repeated logical state preparations and measurements under fixed-rate versus adaptive syndrome extraction schedules using statistical analysis to assess fidelity improvement.

NOVELTY:  
This is the first experimentally validated protocol that integrates *real-time*, experimentally accessible diagnostics of coherence and correlated noise into a closed-loop adaptive syndrome extraction frequency controller on NISQ devices, explicitly addressing measurement backaction and classical latency—moving beyond prior conceptual or simulation-only adaptive QEC schemes and fixed-rate, Markovian noise assumptions.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Systematically characterize and optimize syndrome extraction frequency in a distance-3 surface code on a superconducting qubit platform with mid-circuit measurement and active reset, explicitly quantifying the tradeoff between logical error suppression and measurement/reset-induced errors to identify a non-trivial optimal extraction cadence.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NISQ-era quantum error correction demonstrations typically fix syndrome extraction intervals without experimentally exploring how varying this frequency affects the balance between reducing accumulated data qubit errors and introducing new errors from imperfect measurements and ancilla resets. Measurement-induced dephasing and reset infidelities create competing noise mechanisms that influence logical error rates, but a rigorous quantitative characterization of this tradeoff in realistic hardware remains absent. Establishing the optimal syndrome extraction cadence is essential for practical QEC optimization under real device constraints.

APPROACH:  
We implement repeated rounds of syndrome extraction on a planar distance-3 surface code with flexible pulse-level control to vary extraction intervals from every QEC cycle up to multiples of cycles, while interleaving Ramsey and spin echo sequences on data qubits to measure measurement-induced dephasing. Ancilla reset fidelity and latency are independently benchmarked through repeated reset-measurement sequences. Logical error rates are determined by repeated logical state preparations, syndrome extraction at varied frequencies, and final logical measurements, enabling a direct quantitative mapping of tradeoffs between error suppression and injection across frequencies.

EXPERIMENT:  
For each chosen syndrome extraction interval, we perform multiple trials of logical state preparation followed by a fixed total number of QEC cycles incorporating syndrome extraction and coherence diagnostics, recording final logical measurement outcomes, syndrome fidelities, ancilla reset performance, and qubit coherence parameters. Control experiments without mid-circuit measurements establish baseline coherence and logical error floors. Logical error rate dependence on syndrome extraction frequency is analyzed to confirm or falsify the presence of an intermediate optimal cadence minimizing logical errors.

NOVELTY:  
This is the first experimental study to rigorously quantify and optimize syndrome extraction frequency on a distance-3 surface code implemented on actual superconducting hardware with mid-circuit measurement and active reset, explicitly incorporating measurement-induced dephasing and imperfect reset effects. Unlike prior fixed-interval or theoretical treatments, this approach integrates pulse-level control and interleaved coherence diagnostics to identify a non-trivial optimal extraction cadence, advancing actionable QEC optimization strategies for realistic NISQ devices.
